content.text: An agent role is the complete specification of what one agent in a multi-agent pipeline is responsible for. It combines a name, a single responsibility statement, an explicit list of what the agent does not do, typed input and output schemas, an allowed tool set, a model selection, and a system prompt derived from all of the above.

A role definition is the source of truth for an agent. When a role is well-defined, the system prompt writes itself, the tests write themselves, and the agent's behaviour is predictable. When a role is poorly defined — vague boundaries, multiple responsibilities, undefined output format — the agent becomes unpredictable and hard to debug.

attrs.alt: Six components of an agent role: name, responsibility, tool set, I/O schema, constraints, model

content.text: The Six Components of a Role Definition
Each component of a role definition serves a specific purpose. Together they fully constrain the agent's behaviour.
Unique identifier for the role in the pipeline
One sentence describing the single task
Does not generate responses, modify tickets
Typed Pydantic models for input and output
Only the tools needed for this role
Model matched to task complexity and cost

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Callable, Any

@dataclass
class RoleDefinition:
    name: str                    # unique identifier
    responsibility: str          # one sentence: what this agent does
    does_not: list[str]          # explicit boundary: what it does NOT do
    input_schema: type           # Pydantic model for input
    output_schema: type          # Pydantic model for output
    tools: list[str]             # tool names the agent may use
    model: str                   # LLM to use
    system_prompt: str           # derived from the above

# Example: Classifier role
class ClassifierInput(BaseModel):
    text: str

class ClassifierOutput(BaseModel):
    from typing import Literal
    category: Literal["technical", "billing", "account", "other"]
    confidence: float

classifier_role = RoleDefinition(
    name="classifier",
    responsibility="Classify support tickets into one of four categories.",
    does_not=[
        "generate responses",
        "retrieve information from external sources",
        "modify ticket content",
    ],
    input_schema=ClassifierInput,
    output_schema=ClassifierOutput,
    tools=[],  # classification needs no tools
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    system_prompt=(
        "You are a support ticket classification specialist.\n"
        "Classify tickets into: technical, billing, account, other.\n"
        "You do NOT: generate responses, retrieve data, modify ticket content."
    ),
)
Translating a Role Definition to a LangGraph Node

A role definition maps directly to three artefacts: a system prompt, a Pydantic output schema, and a node function. Each follows mechanically from the role definition — no design decisions remain once the role is fully specified.

from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import TypedDict, Literal
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic

# Step 1: Define I/O schemas
class SummaryInput(BaseModel):
    document: str
    max_sentences: int = 3

class SummaryOutput(BaseModel):
    summary: str
    key_points: list[str]
    word_count: int

# Step 2: Write the system prompt from the role definition
SUMMARISER_SYSTEM_PROMPT = (
    "You are a document summarisation specialist.\n"
    "Given a document, produce a concise summary and list of key points.\n"
    "Rules:\n"
    "- Summary must be at most max_sentences sentences\n"
    "- Key points: 3-5 bullet items, each one concrete fact\n"
    "- Do NOT analyse, interpret, or make recommendations\n"
    "- Do NOT retrieve external information"
)

# Step 3: Implement the node function
class PipelineState(TypedDict):
    document: str
    summary: str
    key_points: list[str]

def summariser_node(state: PipelineState) -> dict:
    """Role: summariser. Responsibility: condense documents."""
    llm = ChatAnthropic(model='claude-haiku-4-5-20251001')
    structured = llm.with_structured_output(SummaryOutput)
    result = structured.invoke([
        {"role": "system", "content": SUMMARISER_SYSTEM_PROMPT},
        {"role": "user", "content": f"Summarise this document:\n{state['document']}"}
    ])
    return {"summary": result.summary, "key_points": result.key_points}

# Step 4: Wire into graph
builder = StateGraph(PipelineState)
builder.add_node('summariser', summariser_node)
builder.set_entry_point('summariser')
builder.add_edge('summariser', END)
graph = builder.compile()

The node function's docstring echoes the role name and responsibility. This makes the codebase self-documenting: any engineer reading the graph definition can see each node's role without opening the system prompt.
A role defines what an agent does. A persona defines how it presents itself. These are orthogonal and can be combined, but keeping them conceptually separate makes both easier to maintain.

# Role vs Persona: key distinction

# PERSONA: how the agent presents itself to a user
# Used in customer-facing agents for tone/brand consistency
PERSONA_PROMPT = (
    "You are Alex, a friendly support assistant for Acme Corp.\n"
    "You are cheerful, patient, and always offer to escalate if you cannot help."
)

# ROLE: what the agent does in a pipeline
# Used for internal pipeline agents — user never sees the system prompt
ROLE_PROMPT = (
    "You are a support ticket classifier.\n"
    "Classify tickets into: technical, billing, account, other.\n"
    "Return structured JSON only. No prose."
)

# An agent can have BOTH: a persona for how it communicates,
# and a role for what it is responsible for
COMBINED_PROMPT = (
    "You are Alex, a friendly support assistant for Acme Corp.\n"
    "Your role is to classify support tickets and generate an initial response.\n"
    "Classification categories: technical, billing, account, other.\n"
    "Tone: warm, professional, never dismissive."
)

# Key difference:
# - Persona changes when branding changes
# - Role changes when pipeline structure changes
# Keeping them separate makes each easier to update independently

Internal pipeline agents — agents that are never directly visible to users — typically need a role but no persona. Customer-facing agents often need both. Conflating the two in a single system prompt makes it harder to update branding without accidentally changing task behaviour.

A role is stable if it does not need to change when neighbouring agents change. The most common stability failure is a role definition that references its neighbours by name, or whose output format is dictated by a downstream agent's expectations.

# Designing roles for stability

from pydantic import BaseModel

# UNSTABLE role definition: depends on neighbouring agents
BAD_SUMMARISER_PROMPT = (
    "You are a summariser. The retriever before you has already fetched documents.\n"
    "Summarise them so the writer after you can draft a report.\n"
    "The writer expects 3 bullet points per document."
)
# Problems:
# - References 'retriever' and 'writer' — breaks if pipeline changes
# - Output format (3 bullets) is determined by the next agent, not this one

# STABLE role definition: self-contained
GOOD_SUMMARISER_PROMPT = (
    "You are a document summarisation specialist.\n"
    "Input: a document.\n"
    "Output: a summary (max 3 sentences) and key_points (3-5 items).\n"
    "You do NOT retrieve, analyse, or write reports."
)

# Stable because:
# - Does not reference other agents by name
# - Output format is defined by the role's own schema, not a downstream agent
# - Replacing the retriever or writer does not require changing this prompt

class StableOutput(BaseModel):
    summary: str
    key_points: list[str]  # always 3-5 items per the schema, not per a neighbour
One sentence, one verb, one task
Explicit "does NOT" list in system prompt
Pydantic model owned by this role
Free-form text, format determined by downstream
Minimal — only tools needed for this role
— "retrieve, summarise, and classify" is three roles. Split it.
— an agent that returns free-form prose cannot be tested in isolation and cannot be safely consumed by the next agent.
— without explicit boundaries, LLMs tend to be helpful beyond their scope, producing output the pipeline is not designed to handle.
— giving an agent access to tools it does not need increases the probability of it using them unexpectedly.
— "step_2" or "second_agent" tells you nothing about the responsibility. Name roles by what they do: classifier, summariser, reviewer.

A well-defined role is directly testable. Each test should verify not just that the agent produces correct output, but that it stays within its role boundaries — it does not call tools it should not, does not produce output in a format other than its schema, and does not perform tasks belonging to other roles.

import pytest
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Literal

class ClassifierOutput(BaseModel):
    category: Literal["technical", "billing", "account", "other"]
    confidence: float

def test_classifier_returns_valid_category():
    mock_llm = MagicMock()
    mock_llm.with_structured_output.return_value.invoke.return_value = \
        ClassifierOutput(category="technical", confidence=0.92)

    state = {'input': 'My API is returning 500 errors'}
    with patch('myagents.classifier.ChatAnthropic', return_value=mock_llm):
        from myagents.classifier import classifier_node
        result = classifier_node(state)

    assert result["category"] == "technical"
    assert 0.0 <= result["confidence"] <= 1.0

def test_classifier_does_not_call_search_tools():
    """Role boundary test: classifier must not attempt retrieval."""
    mock_llm = MagicMock()
    mock_llm.with_structured_output.return_value.invoke.return_value = \
        ClassifierOutput(category="billing", confidence=0.85)

    with patch('myagents.classifier.ChatAnthropic', return_value=mock_llm):
        from myagents.classifier import classifier_node
        classifier_node({'input': 'I was charged twice'})

    # Only one LLM call should have occurred — no tool calls
    mock_llm.invoke.assert_not_called()  # direct .invoke bypasses structured output
    mock_llm.with_structured_output.return_value.invoke.assert_called_once()

def test_classifier_output_schema_is_complete():
    """Schema test: output must match the role's defined schema."""
    mock_llm = MagicMock()
    mock_llm.with_structured_output.return_value.invoke.return_value = \
        ClassifierOutput(category="account", confidence=0.77)

    with patch('myagents.classifier.ChatAnthropic', return_value=mock_llm):
        from myagents.classifier import classifier_node
        result = classifier_node({'input': 'I cannot log in'})

    assert "category" in result
    assert "confidence" in result
    assert result["category"] in ["technical", "billing", "account", "other"]

Boundary tests catch a specific class of bug: an LLM being 'helpful' beyond its assigned scope. A classifier that occasionally writes a draft response has a boundary violation that only shows up in boundary tests, not correctness tests.

In a system with many agents, storing role definitions in a central registry prevents duplication and makes it easy to audit all roles in one place. Any graph can look up a role by name instead of hardcoding system prompts.

Role definitions change for one reason: the rules for their specific task change. When a role is well-defined, that change is isolated — update the system prompt and output schema for that one role, run its tests, and redeploy. No neighbouring agent needs to change.

# Evolving a role definition without breaking the pipeline

# Original classifier role — works but misses an important category
CLASSIFIER_V1 = (
    "You are a ticket classifier.\n"
    "Categories: technical, billing, account, other."
)

# v1.1: Added 'escalation' category for urgent tickets
# Change is isolated to this one system prompt
CLASSIFIER_V1_1 = (
    "You are a ticket classifier.\n"
    "Categories: technical, billing, account, escalation, other.\n"
    "Use escalation for: legal threats, exec contacts, enterprise churn risk."
)

# v1.2: Added confidence threshold — low-confidence tickets go to human review
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Literal

class ClassifierOutputV1(BaseModel):
    category: Literal["technical", "billing", "account", "other"]
    confidence: float

class ClassifierOutputV2(BaseModel):
    category: Literal["technical", "billing", "account", "escalation", "other"]
    confidence: float
    needs_human_review: bool  # True when confidence < 0.7

# The router node checks needs_human_review — only router changes, not other agents
def route_after_classification(state: dict) -> str:
    if state.get("needs_human_review"):
        return "human_review_queue"
    return state.get("category", "other") + "_handler"

Version the output schema alongside the system prompt. When a new field is added (like `needs_human_review`), it is a schema change — downstream agents that consume the output must be updated to use it. The schema version is part of the role contract.
A role definition is complete when you can answer three questions without looking at the code: (1) What does this agent do? (2) What does it not do? (3) What does its output look like?

A well-defined agent role consists of six components: a unique name, a single-sentence responsibility, an explicit 'does NOT' boundary list, typed Pydantic I/O schemas, a minimal tool set, and a matched model. The role definition is the source of truth from which the system prompt, unit tests, and graph node all derive. Roles should be stable — self-contained and free of references to neighbouring agents — so that changing one part of a pipeline does not cascade into changing unrelated roles.
